heading: View X.509 Certificates for a Database User
body:
To list all Atlas-managed, unexpired certificates for a database user using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers certs list <username> [options]

```To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dbusers certs list](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-certs-list).To update a database user from your project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers update <username> [options]

```To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dbusers update](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-update).To delete a database user from your project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers delete <username> [options]

```To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dbusers delete](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-delete).Create database users to provide clients access to the clusters in your
project.A database user's access is determined by the roles assigned
to the user. When you create a database user, any of the [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
add the user to all clusters in your Atlas project.
To specify which resources a database user can access in your project, you
can select the option Restrict Access to Specific Clusters in the Atlas UI
or set [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
and [custom roles](mongodb-roles).Database users are separate from Atlas users. Database users have
access to MongoDB databases, while Atlas users have access to the
Atlas application itself. Atlas supports creating temporary
database users that automatically expire within a user-configurable
7-day period.Atlas audits the creation, deletion, and updates of database users
in the project's Activity Feed. Atlas audits actions pertaining to
both temporary and non-temporary database users.To view the project's Activity Feed:For more information on the project Activity Feed, see
[Activity Feed](project-activity-feed).Atlas supports a maximum of 100 database users per Atlas
project by default. If you require more than 100 database users on a
project, you can use the [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-setgrouplimit) to increase the limit. For assistance, contact
[Atlas support](https://cloud.mongodb.com/support).> **Important:** You must use the [Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create),
> [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-database-users),
> Atlas UI, or a supported [integration](partner-integrations) to add, modify, or delete database users on
> Atlas clusters. Otherwise, Atlas rolls back
> any user modifications.Atlas offers the following forms of authentication for database users:> **Note:** Starting with MongoDB 8.0, LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authentication and authorization is
> deprecated. The feature is available and will continue to operate
> without changes throughout the lifetime of MongoDB 8. LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) will be
> removed in a future major release.
>
> For details, see [LDAP Deprecation](manual/core/LDAP-deprecation).You can create a database user that uses [LDAP authentication and authorization](ldaps-authentication-authorization)
for authentication.The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol)-authenticated users is the `$external` database.If you [LDAP authentication and authorization](ldaps-authentication-authorization), you
can't connect to your clusters with users that authenticate with an
Atlas-managed X.509 certificate. To enable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) and
connecting to your clusters with X.509 users, see
[self-managed X.509 certificate](self-managed-x509).To add database users, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>),
[Organization Stream Processing Admin](<Organization Stream Processing Admin>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), [Project Stream Processing Owner](<Project Stream Processing Owner>), or
[Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access to Atlas.The Atlas CLI uses the following commands to create new database users and X.509 certificates. The options you specify determine the authentication method.To create a database user for your project using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers create [builtInRole]... [options]

```To create a new Atlas-managed X.509 certificate for the specified database user using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers certs create [options]

```To learn more about the syntax and parameters for the previous commands, see the Atlas CLI documentation for
[atlas dbusers create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create) and
[atlas dbusers certs create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-certs-create).Related Links* [Install the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/install-atlas-cli)

* [Connect to the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/connect-atlas-cli)
